reason: The item cleanly targets the boundary between water mist as a propagation-prevention measure versus as an intervention to stop an ongoing runaway, and most correct options stay within that admissibility scope. Decoys are plausible and hinge on common reasoning errors (numerical comparison at onset; overgeneralized rejection), though the multi-correct format plus some overlapping overreach-style errors modestly reduce discrimination and decoy single-fault purity.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility around heat-transfer-mode interpretation, non-monotonic severity metrics, and limits on extrapolation across thickness and SOC, with four defensible correct choices. Decoys are largely single-fault and technically plausible (linear extrapolation past the no-propagation regime; conflating mass stability with performance; safety recommendation from delay-only), yielding strong discrimination against overconfident engineering inference. Minor weakness is that some correct options (A, D) rely on interpretive mechanism language (“consistent with”) that may feel slightly more permissive than strictly necessary, and option verbosity/hedging differs enough to modestly cue test-wise readers.

The item cleanly targets admissible inference from given SOC-dependent propagation/temperature/activation-energy trends plus a second-study pathway contrast, with most correct options framed as consistency/suggestion rather than hard causation. Decoys are largely plausible and fail for identifiable single reasons (linearization, over-prioritizing one metric, or regime transfer), creating real expert discrimination. Minor ambiguity remains around mechanistic interpretation breadth (e.g., mass loss ↔ energy content, pathway sensitivity language), but not enough to undermine overall diagnostic value.

The prompt cleanly targets a single expert judgment: why propagation delay alone is an incomplete safety metric and how a conduction-to-radiation mode shift can increase peak rise rate with a thin barrier. However, as a SHORT_ANSWER item it has no decoy options, making the decoy-craft and option-audit dimensions non-applicable and severely limiting diagnostic separation (many partially-correct responses could score similarly without a structured way to distinguish failure modes). Revise by converting to multi-option or adding a structured rubric subpart breakdown (e.g., explicitly require two distinct limitations + a mechanistic explanation) to strengthen discriminative signal.
